function.description:
Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.

start_index.description: On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index.title: Start Index

max_length.description: Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length.title: Max Length

raw.description: Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw.title: Raw

function.name: fetch

function.description: Run a process on this linux machine

timeout_ms.description: Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms

stdin_text.description: Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.

command_line.description: Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.

argv.description: Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.

function.name: run_process

function.description: Execute arbitrary Lua code in the Neovim process. Useful for calling vim API functions like vim.notify or vim.cmd.

code.description: Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk.

function.name: run_lua

patch.description: file changes in custom diff format

messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/dotfiles

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Fetch Tool

- Use repo's README `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/evals/refs/heads/main/README.md` instead of `https://github.com/openai/evals`.
- Prefer raw content like `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/g0t4/dotfiles/refs/heads/master/install/symlinks.fish` instead of `https://github.com/g0t4/dotfiles/blob/master/install/symlinks.fish`
- When relevant, prefer github CLI: `gh pr list` or `gh issue view 12`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="gptoss120b<wes.mcclure+gptoss120b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply.

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file operations ]
*** End Patch

Each “operation” MUST start with one of three “headers”:

*** Add File: <path> - every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - in-place edits (optionally with a rename).

UpdateFile can be immediately followed by MoveTo to rename the file:
*** Move to: <new_path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).

Within a hunk each line starts with:
+ for inserted text,
- for removed text, or
  space (" ") for context (unchanged lines to match)

At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit:
*** End of File

### Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

### A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

### Reminders

- Verify your changes with `git diff foo.json`
- Nothing wrong with commands too:
  - `rm foo.json` to delete
  - `mv foo.json bar.json` to rename
  - `rename` for bulk rename
- Don't be that guy that uses DeleteFile followed by AddFile just to rename a file!


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Fish Shell Code Preferences

- Use snake_case for functions and variables.
- When a condition is ambiguous, assign it to a well‑named variable before the `if`.
- Prefer long options, i.e. `--quiet` over `-q`, `--regex` over `-r`
- Fish shell does not do word splitting like bash, no need to wrap everything in quotes!

## Fish syntax examples

```fish
# exploit fish-isms!

# use `not`, avoid `!`
if not test -f spoon
    echo There is no spoon
    exit 1
end

# if + set
if set -l message (some_command)
    echo "ok: $message"
else
    echo "fail: $message"
end

# prefer named arguments
function show_variable --argument-names name value
    echo "$name = $value"
end

# fish doesn't have heredocs, use begin/end
begin
    echo $header
    echo $table
end > out.html
# or multi-line strings
echo "
foo
bar
" > test

# every variable is an array
set names wes john bob jane
for name in $names
    echo $name
end

for i in *.tmp
    if grep DONE $i
        break
    end
    if grep SKIP $i
        continue
    end
    ...
end

# combine conditions
while test -f foo.txt; or test -f bar.txt
    ...
end

# conditional execution
make; and make install; or make clean
```

### builtin fish commands

Now that we have more than 2KB of system RAM, we can fix most of what sucks about bash...

```fish
# *** string command

# match: test, filter, extract
string match --quiet '*.lua' $file
set lua_files (string match '*.lua' $files)
set version (string match --regex 'v([0-9.]+)' $tag)
string match --index --regex 'ERROR' $line

# replace: transform text
set fixed (string replace foo bar $text)
set cleaned (string replace --regex '\s+' ' ' $line)
set base (string replace --regex '\.[^.]+$' '' $file)
set normalized (string replace --filter --regex '^./' '' $paths)

# split: tokenize
set parts (string split ':' $PATH)
set lines (string split '\n' $text)
set ext (string split --right --max 1 . $file)[2]

# trim: clean edges
set clean (string trim $line)
set unquoted (string trim --chars '"' $s)

# join: build strings
string join ' ' $argv
string join --no-empty ':' $PATH

# length and slicing
string length $name
string sub --start 2 --length 4 $word
string sub --start -5 $word

# case conversion
set key (string lower $key)
set name (string upper $name)

# shell-safe encoding
set escaped (string escape $path)
string unescape $escaped

# normalize piped output
set text (some_command | string collect)


# path command
path basename foo/bar.txt # bar.txt
path basename --no-extension foo/bar.txt # bar
path dirname foo/bar.txt # foo
path extension ./foo.mp4 # .mp4
path change-extension mov ./foo.mp4 # ./foo.mov

path normalize path/to//hello.txt # path/to/hello.txt
path normalize path/to/../hello.txt # path/hello.txt
# resolve = normalize + absolute path and follow symlinks
path resolve foo  # ~/foo

path sort 10-foo 2-bar
# 2-bar
# 10-foo


# math command
math [(-s | --scale) N] [(-b | --base) BASE] [(-m | --scale-mode) MODE] EXPRESSION ...
math 10 / 6
math $status - 128
math "5 * 2"
math "sin(pi)"
math max 5,2,3,1


# count STRING1 STRING2 ...
# COMMAND | count
# count [...] < FILE
count $PATH
count *.txt
git ls-files --others --exclude-standard | count
printf '%s\n' foo bar | count baz # Returns 3 (2 lines from stdin plus 1 argument)
count < /etc/hosts # number of entries in hosts file


# contains [OPTIONS] KEY [VALUES ...]
if contains -- -q $argv
    echo '$argv contains a -q option'
end

# scripts
status filename # current running script
status basename
status dirname
status function # current function name
status line-number
status stack-trace

```

messages.reasoning_content: The file defines _fzf_nested_file_picker using fd --type f . which by default excludes symlinks? fd default follows symlinks? According to fd docs, by default follows symlinks when -L? Actually fd by default does not follow symlinks for directories, but includes symlinked files? Not sure. Might be that symlinked files are omitted because fd excludes hidden? Let's check fd options. The file shows unrestricted version uses -u to include hidden, but still symlinks maybe not. To include symlinks we need fd --type f -H? Actually fd has options: -L to follow symlinks, -a to include hidden and ignored. Let's search for symlink mention.

messages.reasoning_content:
We need to modify file picker to include symlinked files. The function __fzf_picker uses fd command passed as argument. For files picker, it's "fd --type f .". To include symlinks, we can add --type f --type symlink or -H? Actually fd supports multiple --type options? It can accept multiple values, maybe --type f or --type symlink. So we modify the file picker definitions to include symlinks. Probably adjust _fzf_nested_file_picker to use fd --type f --type symlink . Or use fd -a? But want only files, not directories. Symlinked files are type symlink but not f. So include both. Let's edit lines where _fzf_nested_file_picker and unrestricted and both. The unrestricted includes -u which includes hidden and maybe symlinks? It still uses --type f . -u, not symlink. So modify to include symlink type.

Patch file: fish/load_last_interactive_only/files-specific.fish around line 878-883.

Let's open snippet.

messages.content:
{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"0","name":"EXIT_CODE"},{"type":"text","text":"end

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

function _fzf_nested_dir_picker -d "Paste selected directory into command line"
    __fzf_picker dirs "fd --type d ."
end

function _fzf_nested_file_picker -d "Paste selected file into command line"
    __fzf_picker files "fd --type f ."
end

function _fzf_nested_file_unrestricted_picker
    __fzf_picker unrestricted "fd --type f . -u"
end

function _fzf_nested_both_file_and_dirs_picker -d "Paste selected file or directory into command line"

__verbose.prompt:
<|start|>system<|message|>You are ChatGPT, a large language model trained by OpenAI.
Knowledge cutoff: 2024-06
Current date: 2026-04-28

Reasoning: medium

# Valid channels: analysis, commentary, final. Channel must be included for every message.
Calls to these tools must go to the commentary channel: 'functions'.<|end|><|start|>developer<|message|># Instructions

You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers using code comments.
- NEVER change **unrelated code and comments.**
- Never add new comments to the end of a line.
- Never add trivial comments.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer readable code over of comments.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/dotfiles

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Fetch Tool

- Use repo's README `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/evals/refs/heads/main/README.md` instead of `https://github.com/openai/evals`.
- Prefer raw content like `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/g0t4/dotfiles/refs/heads/master/install/symlinks.fish` instead of `https://github.com/g0t4/dotfiles/blob/master/install/symlinks.fish`
- When relevant, prefer github CLI: `gh pr list` or `gh issue view 12`


## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="gptoss120b<wes.mcclure+gptoss120b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.


## Use `apply_patch` to edit files.

Your patch language is a stripped‑down, file‑oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply.

*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file operations ]
*** End Patch

Each “operation” MUST start with one of three “headers”:

*** Add File: <path> - every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
*** Delete File: <path> - remove an existing file. Nothing follows.
*** Update File: <path> - in-place edits (optionally with a rename).

UpdateFile can be immediately followed by MoveTo to rename the file:
*** Move to: <new_path>

Then one or more “hunks”, each introduced by @@ (optionally followed by a hunk header).

Within a hunk each line starts with:
+ for inserted text,
- for removed text, or
  space (" ") for context (unchanged lines to match)

At the end of a truncated hunk you can emit:
*** End of File

### Here is the grammar:

Patch := Begin { FileOp } End
Begin := "*** Begin Patch" NEWLINE
End := "*** End Patch" NEWLINE
FileOp := AddFile | DeleteFile | UpdateFile
AddFile := "*** Add File: " path NEWLINE { "+" line NEWLINE }
DeleteFile := "*** Delete File: " path NEWLINE
UpdateFile := "*** Update File: " path NEWLINE [ MoveTo ] { Hunk }
MoveTo := "*** Move to: " newPath NEWLINE
Hunk := "@@" [ header ] NEWLINE { HunkLine } [ "*** End of File" NEWLINE ]
HunkLine := (" " | "-" | "+") text NEWLINE

### A full patch can combine several operations:

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
*** Move to: src/main.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch

### Reminders

- Verify your changes with `git diff foo.json`
- Nothing wrong with commands too:
  - `rm foo.json` to delete
  - `mv foo.json bar.json` to rename
  - `rename` for bulk rename
- Don't be that guy that uses DeleteFile followed by AddFile just to rename a file!


Tips on a failure:

- You only need enough context for a unique match!
- The line(s) you replace are context too; if they’re unique, no additional context is needed.
- Split bigger patches into smaller patches, i.e. if editing two functions, try each separately!
- Watch out for whitespace differences, i.e. blank lines

Please be curteous:

- NEVER leave comments about removed code, just get rid of it. This code lives in a git repo.
- Check your work! Especially after a failure.

### More Examples

Here's a patch file to change the `win` property's type:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@ @dataclass
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
-    gap_rms: TimeSeries
-    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
+    gap_rms: TimeSeries
+    analysis: AudioAnalysisResult
*** End Patch
```

Which can be simplified to:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-class GapContext:
-    win: GapWindow
+class GapContext:
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```

Or, this could work if the `win` property is unique:

```diff
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: src/gap.py
@@
-    win: GapWindow
+    win: SilenceWindow
*** End Patch
```


# Tools

## functions

namespace functions {

// Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that.
type fetch = (_: {
// On return output starting at this character index, useful if a previous fetch was truncated and more context is required.
start_index?: number, // default: 0,
// Maximum number of characters to return.
max_length?: number, // default: 5000,
// URL to fetch
url: string,
// Get the actual HTML content of the requested page, without simplification.
raw?: boolean, // default: false,
}) => any;

// Run a process on this linux machine
type run_process = (_: {
// Optional timeout in milliseconds, defaults to 30,000ms
timeout_ms?: number,
// Optional text written to STDIN (written fully, then closed). Useful for heredoc-style input or file contents.
stdin_text?: string,
// Shell mode: a shell command line executed via the system's default shell. Supports pipes, redirects, globbing. Cannot be combined with 'argv'.
command_line?: string,
// Optional to set working directory
cwd?: string,
// Executable mode: directly spawn a process. argv[0] is the executable, followed by arguments passed verbatim (no shell interpretation). Cannot be combined with 'command_line'.
argv?: string[],
}) => any;

// Execute arbitrary Lua code in the Neovim process. Useful for calling vim API functions like vim.notify or vim.cmd.
type run_lua = (_: {
// Lua code to execute. Should be a valid Lua chunk.
code: string,
}) => any;

// Patch a file
type apply_patch = (_: {
// file changes in custom diff format
patch: string,
}) => any;

} // namespace functions<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>## General Code Preferences

- When rewriting code, leave unrelated code and unrelated comments as is.
- When an if statement has an AMBIGUOUS condition, extract a variable to meaningfully name it, for example:
```lua
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```
<|end|><|start|>user<|message|>## Fish Shell Code Preferences

- Use snake_case for functions and variables.
- When a condition is ambiguous, assign it to a well‑named variable before the `if`.
- Prefer long options, i.e. `--quiet` over `-q`, `--regex` over `-r`
- Fish shell does not do word splitting like bash, no need to wrap everything in quotes!

## Fish syntax examples

```fish
# exploit fish-isms!

# use `not`, avoid `!`
if not test -f spoon
    echo There is no spoon
    exit 1
end

# if + set
if set -l message (some_command)
    echo "ok: $message"
else
    echo "fail: $message"
end

# prefer named arguments
function show_variable --argument-names name value
    echo "$name = $value"
end

# fish doesn't have heredocs, use begin/end
begin
    echo $header
    echo $table
end > out.html
# or multi-line strings
echo "
foo
bar
" > test

# every variable is an array
set names wes john bob jane
for name in $names
    echo $name
end

for i in *.tmp
    if grep DONE $i
        break
    end
    if grep SKIP $i
        continue
    end
    ...
end

# combine conditions
while test -f foo.txt; or test -f bar.txt
    ...
end

# conditional execution
make; and make install; or make clean
```

### builtin fish commands

Now that we have more than 2KB of system RAM, we can fix most of what sucks about bash...

```fish
# *** string command

# match: test, filter, extract
string match --quiet '*.lua' $file
set lua_files (string match '*.lua' $files)
set version (string match --regex 'v([0-9.]+)' $tag)
string match --index --regex 'ERROR' $line

# replace: transform text
set fixed (string replace foo bar $text)
set cleaned (string replace --regex '\s+' ' ' $line)
set base (string replace --regex '\.[^.]+$' '' $file)
set normalized (string replace --filter --regex '^./' '' $paths)

# split: tokenize
set parts (string split ':' $PATH)
set lines (string split '\n' $text)
set ext (string split --right --max 1 . $file)[2]

# trim: clean edges
set clean (string trim $line)
set unquoted (string trim --chars '"' $s)

# join: build strings
string join ' ' $argv
string join --no-empty ':' $PATH

# length and slicing
string length $name
string sub --start 2 --length 4 $word
string sub --start -5 $word

# case conversion
set key (string lower $key)
set name (string upper $name)

# shell-safe encoding
set escaped (string escape $path)
string unescape $escaped

# normalize piped output
set text (some_command | string collect)


# path command
path basename foo/bar.txt # bar.txt
path basename --no-extension foo/bar.txt # bar
path dirname foo/bar.txt # foo
path extension ./foo.mp4 # .mp4
path change-extension mov ./foo.mp4 # ./foo.mov

path normalize path/to//hello.txt # path/to/hello.txt
path normalize path/to/../hello.txt # path/hello.txt
# resolve = normalize + absolute path and follow symlinks
path resolve foo  # ~/foo

path sort 10-foo 2-bar
# 2-bar
# 10-foo


# math command
math [(-s | --scale) N] [(-b | --base) BASE] [(-m | --scale-mode) MODE] EXPRESSION ...
math 10 / 6
math $status - 128
math "5 * 2"
math "sin(pi)"
math max 5,2,3,1


# count STRING1 STRING2 ...
# COMMAND | count
# count [...] < FILE
count $PATH
count *.txt
git ls-files --others --exclude-standard | count
printf '%s\n' foo bar | count baz # Returns 3 (2 lines from stdin plus 1 argument)
count < /etc/hosts # number of entries in hosts file


# contains [OPTIONS] KEY [VALUES ...]
if contains -- -q $argv
    echo '$argv contains a -q option'
end

# scripts
status filename # current running script
status basename
status dirname
status function # current function name
status line-number
status stack-trace

```

response_message.content:
Implemented updates to the FZF file pickers to include symlinked files:

- Modified `_fzf_nested_file_picker` to use `fd --type f --type symlink`.
- Updated `_fzf_nested_file_unrestricted_picker` to include symlinks with `fd --type f --type symlink`.
- Adjusted `_fzf_nested_both_file_and_dirs_picker` to include symlinks for both files and directories.

These changes ensure symlinked files appear in the FZF selections. Commit applied.
